flashcards.Back: A 128-bit logical identifier typically represented as eight groups of four hexadecimal digits (quartets) separated by colons.
A 16-bit segment of an IPv6 address, written as four hexadecimal digits. There are eight quartets in total in a full IPv6 address.
An IPv6 abbreviation rule that allows the removal of any leading zeros within a 16-bit quartet (e.g., '00AB' becomes 'AB').
An IPv6 rule that allows replacing a contiguous sequence of all-zero quartets with a double colon. This can only be applied once per address.
The leading bits of an IPv6 address that define the network or subnet. it is analogous to the network portion of an IPv4 address.
A decimal value following a slash (e.g., /64) that indicates how many of the leading bits in an IPv6 address represent the network prefix.
The specific IPv6 address block assigned to an organization by an ISP or RIR, typically making up the first 48 bits.
A 16-bit field in a standard /64 address structure that follows the global routing prefix, used by an organization to identify its internal subnets.
The 64-bit portion of an IPv6 address that uniquely identifies a specific interface on a subnet. it occupies the last half of the address.
Stateless Address Autoconfiguration. A mechanism allowing hosts to configure their own IPv6 address and gateway using ICMPv6 Router Advertisements.
An NDP message sent periodically by routers to provide hosts with the local network prefix, length, and default gateway information.
An NDP message sent by a host upon joining a network to immediately request configuration info from any local routers rather than waiting for an RA.
A suite of ICMPv6 messages that replaces ARP, performing functions like MAC address resolution and router discovery.
A mandatory IPv6 process where a host verifies that its newly generated address is not already in use by another device on the same link.
A method of automatically creating a 64-bit interface ID by expanding a 48-bit MAC address and inverting the 7th bit.
A full 32-digit hexadecimal representation of an IPv6 address with no leading zeros removed and no zero compression applied.
Zeros at the end of an IPv6 quartet. Unlike leading zeros, these can never be removed as it would change the value of the address.
Another term for the Subnet ID or the network portion of the address that identifies the specific logical segment.
The reachability limit of an address with the fe80::/10 prefix. Traffic with these addresses never leaves the local network segment.
An NDP message used to request the MAC address of a neighbor or to verify a neighbor's reachability, replacing the ARP Request.
An NDP message sent in response to an NS, providing the requested MAC address, analogous to an ARP Reply.
A specialized multicast address used in NDP to efficiently resolve MAC addresses without broadcasting to every node on the link.
A reserved IPv6 address that targets every IPv6-enabled device on the local link, similar to an IPv4 broadcast.
A reserved IPv6 address used to communicate with all routers on a segment, primarily for sending Router Solicitations.
A special anycast address automatically assigned to all router interfaces on a subnet, typically using the subnet's prefix with an all-zero interface ID.
The version of Internet Control Message Protocol for IPv6. it handles error reporting, diagnostics, and essential neighbor discovery functions.
A mechanism where a router automatically receives a network prefix from an upstream provider to use for its own internal subnets.
The manual assignment of an IPv6 address and prefix length to an interface using the 'ipv6 address' command.
The use of 'show ipv6 interface' or 'show ipv6 route' commands to confirm that addressing and reachability are correctly configured.
The state where a host can communicate across the public internet using a Global Unicast Address and valid routing entries.
